summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from human anatomy and evolution, to career transitions into data science, youth sports parenting dilemmas, and career change advice. The most substantive advice concerns a data engineer wanting to break into data science, where she recommends building a portfolio with public data and getting hired as an engineer at a data-driven company to 'worm your way' into the data team. She also offers strongly-worded parenting advice against the competitive youth sports culture, advocating for giving children agency over their free time activities.
key_points: For career changers into data science: build projects with public data as a portfolio, then get hired as an engineer at a data company and prove your skills internally
Competitive youth sports culture is criticized as parents projecting existential anxiety onto children's activities
Children should have agency over their free time rather than having adults define their goals
Wanting to pursue a field you have no experience in may signal escapism rather than genuine desire
The advice column format allows for frank, sometimes profane commentary on social norms
money_quotes: My impression is that no man has ever gotten that close to sex and then said, 'whoa, your vagina has a slightly peculiar shape and/or positioning relative to your clitoris. Maybe we should not procreate after all!!'
Try to get a job as an engineer in a data-driven business, and worm your way into the data group.
Literally, people seem to think that their kid will get into Harvard on the strength of their parents' SERIOUS COMMITMENT to youth sports.
It's even offensive to think that adults should define the goal for growing children in their free time.
What's wrong with you people, isn't it enough that these kids will probably have to live by other people's rules when they're working in jobs later? Why do we have to start that crap so soon?
Be consistently sane and give them absolute agency on these decisions.
How would you know you want to do math research if you don't have experience in math?
I'm sensing more escapism than desire in your words.
key_insight: Whether in career transitions or parenting decisions, honest self-assessment and giving people agency over their own paths matters more than following prescribed formulas for success.
